patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the btrfs_log_dev_io_error() function to log device I/O errors for all bio error status codes, not just BLK_STS_IOERR and BLK_STS_TARGET. The change is motivated by the observation that some block layer drivers return unexpected status values, leading to incorrect conclusions about system behavior.

[PATCH v2 5/5] btrfs: cap shrink_delalloc iterations to 128M

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch limits the iterations of the `shrink_delalloc` function in Btrfs to prevent it from blocking indefinitely, especially when dealing with large amounts of writeback data. The function now iterates over delalloc work in units of 128MiB, rather than trying to reclaim all available space at once. This change aims to strike a balance between making progress on delalloc and not starving other types of reservations, which can lead to system stalls and long commit times.

[PATCH v2 4/5] btrfs: make inode->outstanding_extents a u64

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch updates the data type of the 'outstanding_extents' field in the Btrfs inode structure from an unsigned integer (u32) to a 64-bit unsigned integer (u64). This change is motivated by the fact that the maximum file size is limited by LLONG_MAX, which is approximately 2^63. As a result, the maximum extent size computation in Btrfs has always been bounded above by this value, making it necessary to use a larger data type to accurately represent this value. The patch also updates several functions and variables to work with the new u64 data type.

[PATCH v2 3/5] btrfs: account for compression in delalloc extent reservation

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses an issue where the btrfs kernel module under-reserved space for delayed reference reservations during delalloc writes when compression is enabled. The problem arises from an incorrect estimate of outstanding extents due to a mismatch between maximum uncompressed and compressed extent sizes. To fix this, the patch introduces new functions in btrfs_inode.h to calculate the maximum extent size based on inode properties and uses these values to accurately reserve space for delayed references. As a result, the patch improves the accuracy of delalloc reservation calculations and prevents under-reservation issues even when compression is forced or always enabled for an inode.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 1/5] btrfs: reserve space for delayed_refs in delalloc

[PATCH v2 1/5] btrfs: reserve space for delayed_refs in delalloc

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a problem where the Btrfs file system does not reserve enough space for delayed references (delayed_refs) during delalloc operations. Delalloc uses a per-inode block reservation to anticipate metadata needs, but it does not account for delayed refs. As a result, when writeback occurs and delayed refs are generated, they draw from an under-reserved trans_handle's delayed_refs_rsv, leading to incorrect assessments of reserved space in the metadata space reclaim logic. The patch fixes this by reserving space for delayed refs during delalloc operations.

Reviewer noted that writing into a prealloc extent can also generate delayed refs, which are not currently reserved in the delalloc logic.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses stalls caused by sudden writeback in Btrfs file systems on large memory systems. The patches improve reservations for delayed refs and smooth out the work in flush_space() to prevent system stalling due to excessive ordered extents and delayed refs blocking metadata space system operations. The changes include updating rsv accounting, cleaning up types counting max extents, reducing the unit of work in shrink_delalloc(), and capping iterations to 128M. These modifications aim to reduce stalls on large systems with high memory and disk capacity.

review_comments.author: Jason Gunthorpe
review_comments.summary: Proposed using the request_resource mechanism to lock CXL ranges in the resource tree, and suggested turning phys_addr_t's into large cachable special PTEs inside a VMA.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Apr 06, 2026 at 05:22:06PM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> On Sat, Apr 04, 2026 at 12:36:53PM -0700, Dan Williams wrote:
> > Jason Gunthorpe wrote:
> > > 
> > > The entire thing needs to be mmapable in a cache coherent way since
> > > that is what the HW semantic is. If you try to do something else you
> > > will break KVM support since it follows the VMA.
> > 
> > Then I assume it matters that memremap() sometimes silently falls back
> > to the direct map. The "VFIO owns" expectation needs to guard against
> > some helpful platform firmware mapping accelerator memory as System RAM.
> > 
> > At a minimum having VFIO fail to map in that case helps with the
> > argument I have been making that "no, EFI_CONVENTIONAL_MEMORY type +
> > EFI_SPECIFIC_PURPOSE flag" is not suitable for accelerators with private
> > CXL memory. Those want to be enforcing "EFI_RESERVED".
> 
> Agree - in fact I would argue any potential user of private nodes should
> have some kind of splat saying the memory should be marked reserved in
> the first place, otherwise it's a firmware bug.

I would expect this to happen via the request_resource mechanism, if
MM is using the CXL range it should be locked in the resource tree and
vfio should request it and then fail during the initial startup
phases.

> I need to read up a little bit on this area, but i don't see the "needs
> to be mmap'able in a cache coherent way" to be an argument for one
> particular method or another (hotplug, private node, memremap, etc).

It is speaking to what VFIO has to do. It is the exclusive owner of
the physical range, it does not have struct pages, it must be cachable
for VFIO and KVM to work - not alot of choices here. Turn the
phys_addr_t's into large cachable special PTEs inside a VMA.

Jason

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] dcache: warn when a dentry is freed with a non-empty ->d_lru

version_history.subject: [PATCH] dcache: warn when a dentry is freed with a non-empty ->d_lru

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a warning to the Linux kernel when a dentry (directory entry) is freed while its 'd_lru' list is not empty. This is done to prevent use-after-free errors, which can cause panics in the system. The problem was identified through a series of panics on hosts with high process churn and was also reported by syzbot. The patch adds a WARN_ON_ONCE() call to the dentry_free function to catch this issue and make it easier to debug.

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/4] mm: improve write performance with RWF_DONTCACHE

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/4] mm: fix IOCB_DONTCACHE write performance with rate-limited writeback
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses performance issues caused by the IOCB_DONTCACHE flag, which triggers aggressive flushing of dirty pages on every write operation. The problem arises when multiple writers contend for writeback submission, leading to severe serialization and reduced throughput. To fix this, a new function filemap_dontcache_writeback_range() is introduced, which uses two rate-limiting mechanisms: skip-if-busy (checking if writeback is already in progress) and proportional cap (limiting the number of pages flushed). These mechanisms prevent excessive flushing and contention between concurrent writers. The patch replaces the existing filemap_flush_range() call with the new function in generic_write_sync().

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged a reviewer's point about potential contention for xa_lock, agreed to investigate further using perf tools.

The author acknowledged a concern about the effectiveness of relying on VM dirty limits when DONTCACHE writers skip flushing and explained that they tried an alternative approach but it performed worse, indicating no immediate plan to revisit this issue.

Author acknowledged that their previous patch contained an error, promised to fix it in a revised version (v2) and will resubmit soon.
review_comments.raw_body:
That's an interesting point.

The theory I've been operating on is that the flusher thread ends up
squatting on the xa_lock for a while when memory gets tight, and that
blocks other readers and writers. Staying ahead of the dirty limits and
limiting the amount of flush work that each writer does alleviates
contention for that lock and that's what improves the performance.

You're right though. I'll plan to play around with perf and see if I
can confirm the theory.

---

Correct. When DONTCACHE writers end up skipping the flush, we rely on
VM dirty limits to eventually take care of flushing the data that got
skipped. That's why the DONTCACHE dirty pagecache max size ends up
looking close to buffered mode's.

I did play with a patch that had the writers attempt to flush 4x more
than they had written when memory was tight to compensate for that, but
it ended up performing worse than this set. It's possible that tuning
that down to 2x or so would do better, but I decided to just stop here
and post what I had.

---

review_comments.raw_body:
But nr_to_write should not change anything.  If .range_start and
.range_end are set in a writeback_iter() loop, writeback_iter will try to
get and writeback every page in the range.  Setting nr_to_write in
addition to that could only reduce the amount written if it was less than

and nr_to_write as that combination doesn't make sense.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/4] mm: add atomic flush guard for IOCB_DONTCACHE writeback
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds an atomic flush guard to prevent a 'thundering herd' problem that causes high tail latency when multiple IOCB_DONTCACHE writers submit flushes simultaneously after the PAGECACHE_TAG_WRITEBACK tag clears. The patch introduces an AS_DONTCACHE_FLUSHING flag and uses test_and_set_bit() to ensure only one writer flushes at a time, while also adding a dirty pressure escape hatch that forces extra flushes when dirty pages exceed 75% of the dirty_ratio threshold.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Christoph Hellwig expressed concern about a potential lock ordering issue and suggested alternative approaches to handling IOCB_DONTCACHE writes, including disabling in-caller writeback or queuing up wb_writeback_work instances.
review_comments.raw_body:
This sounds like a bad reimplementation of the single writeback thread
:)

Have you considered stopping to do in-caller writeback for
IOCB_DONTCACHE vs just leaving it to the writeback daeon?

Either by totally disabling the writeback and just leaving the
dropbehind bit, or by queuing up wb_writeback_work instances for
the ranges, or by just increasing the pressure for the writeback
daemon.  Note that with all schemes including the one in this patch
we might eventually run into writeback scalability limits, which
will require multiple writeback workers.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the current implementation of 'dropbehind' mode is not sufficient to address tail latency issues, and plans to explore a new approach where each writer submits writeback work without lock contention, potentially benefiting both IOCB_DONTCACHE and normal buffered writes.
review_comments.raw_body:
I did test a "dropbehind" mode that just set the dropbehind bit without
doing the flush at the end of the write. It was better than stock
dontcache but the tail latencies were still pretty bad.

I think having each writer do some writeback submission work makes a
lot of sense. It helps keep the dirty pages below the dirty thresholds
and doesn't seem to tax each writing task _too_ much. The trick is
avoiding lock contention while doing it.

I think what would be ideal would be to have some (lockless) mechanism
to say "there is enough data touched by the range just written to kick
off a write that's a suitable size for the backing store". Each writer
could check that and then kick off writeback for an approprite range.

I think this even could be beneficial in the normal buffered write
codepath too.

Anyway, I'll play around with this idea some more and come back with a
v2.

Thanks for the review!
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the patch introduces shared resource contention between IOCB_DONTCACHE writers, which can lead to scalability issues, and suggested avoiding such contention by using a single thread for writeback operations.

The reviewer suggested queuing up writeback on the writeback thread for each dontcache write and proposed an initial approximation of queuing a wb_writeback_work for each range, but recommended finding a more efficient way to increase a threshold instead.

Reviewer noted that the 30-second timeout for writeback can be actively harmful, especially on SSDs and HDDs, and suggested considering a shorter or dynamic timeout.
review_comments.raw_body:
Well, an any time you hit a shared resources from multiple threads you
create that lock contention.   Which is why in file system and writeback
land we've moved away from random user processes hitting both data and
metadata (e.g. XFS AIL) writeback as it leads to these scalability
issues.  At some point we might run out of steam in a single thread,
although so far that's mostly been because it does stupid things
(e.g. writeback on file systems doing complex allocator stuff).

---

And that is called the writeback thread.  So what we should do there
is to make sure we queue up writeback on it for each dontcache write.
Initially queuing up a wb_writeback_work for each range might be first
approximation, although we should probably find a way to just increase
a threshold if going down that road.

---

Yes, we've had lots of observation that the current 30s timeout is
actively harmful.  Especially on SSDs, but even on HDD just keeping
the active might make sense.

Ok, I like that idea. I'll give that a shot and see how it does. I'll
note that there is no way to specify an inode or range (yet) in
wb_writeback_work().

Do you think it's sufficient to just call something like
wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi() after every RWF_DONTCACHE write, or should
I extend wb_writeback_work to allow for doing work on a range within a
single inode?

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that IOCB_DONTCACHE writes don't require integrity operations and suggested waking the flusher thread instead of implementing atomic flush guard, also requesting a way to communicate higher writeback targets.

I don't think we care about what exact data is written back as this is
not an integrity operation.  So just waking the flusher thread sounds
like the right thing, but we might also need a way to communicate
the higher writeback target.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/4] testing: add nfsd-io-bench NFS server benchmark suite
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a benchmark suite called nfsd-io-bench to test the performance of NFSD I/O modes using fio and the libnfs backend against an NFS server on localhost. The suite includes tests for buffered, don'tcache, and direct I/O modes via NFSD debugfs controls. It also includes scripts to orchestrate the test matrix, parse results from fio JSON output, and configure the NFS export for testing. This patch aims to provide a comprehensive benchmarking tool for evaluating NFSD I/O performance.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/4] testing: add dontcache-bench local filesystem benchmark suite
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new benchmark suite called 'dontcache-bench' to test the IOCB_DONTCACHE flag on local filesystems using fio's io_uring engine. The suite includes various job files and scripts to run and parse the results of these tests, mirroring the existing nfsd-io-bench test matrix but with different I/O modes. The goal is to evaluate the performance impact of disabling caching for I/O operations on local filesystems.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patchset improves write performance when using the RWF_DONTCACHE flag by addressing flush contention issues that occur during heavy write activity. The patches modify the Linux kernel's mm module to alleviate this contention, resulting in better performance for writes. The changes were tested with various workloads and IO modes, including buffered, direct, and unpatched dontcache, and showed significant improvements over the original RWF_DONTCACHE implementation. The patchset also includes benchmarking scripts to demonstrate the results of these tests.

Reviewer Ritesh suspects that the 2.5x performance penalty in sequential writes is due to contention between the submission side and completion side taking the xa_lock, which allows the writer to do more work but causes a bottleneck on the completion callback holding folio_end_dropbehind().

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v2] nfsd: fix file change detection in CB_GETATTR
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch fixes an issue where file change detection was not working correctly in the NFS daemon's (nfsd) CB_GETATTR operation, which is used to retrieve attributes of a file. review_comments.author: Scott Mayhew
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Scott Mayhew pointed out that the pseudocode in section 10.4.3 of the documentation only checks the change attribute to determine if a file has been modified, but then later mentions checking the file size, which is inconsistent and potentially misleading.

Reviewer Scott Mayhew pointed out that comparing the mtime is not specified in the spec and suggested that a different mtime from the server's would be a client bug, also expressing concern about crossing into delegated timestamps territory
review_comments.raw_body:
You're right, it's not.  I dislike how the pseudocode in 10.4.3 is only
checking the change attribute to determine if the file has been modified
and then 2 paragraphs down it talks about checking the file size.

At any rate, the main point of this fix is to make sure we're comparing
the size from the cb_getattr reply to the current size, not a cached
value.  For example:

client 1 does open(O_RDWR|O_CREATE_O_TRUNC)
client 1 writes some data and flushes it to the server
client 2 does some operation that triggers a CB_GETATTR

At this point client 1 doesn't have any modified data, so it sends the
original change attribute and its current file size (which should match
the current file size on the server).  With the current code, the server
compares the size client 1 sent with the value it cached on the
delegation (in this case 0).  The server treats it as if the client is
has modified data, which it does not.  That's all this patch is aiming
to fix.

---

But the spec doesn't say anything about comparing the mtime.  Plus, if
the client is sending the original change attribute but a different
mtime than what the server has, wouldn't that be a client bug?

Plus, wouldn't it sort of be crossing into delegated timestamps
territory if we started checking the mtime?

series_items.subject: [RFC 1/2] mm: page_alloc: replace pageblock_flags bitmap with struct pageblock_data
series_items.patch_summary: This patch replaces the packed pageblock_flags bitmap with a per-pageblock struct containing its own flags word, reducing cache line contention and simplifying accessor code. series_items.subject: [RFC 2/2] mm: page_alloc: per-cpu pageblock buddy allocator
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a per-cpu pageblock buddy allocator to address scalability issues in large machines due to zone->lock contention. It extends the per-cpu (PCP) allocator to operate on whole pageblocks, allowing each CPU to claim and split them locally, eliminating affinity violations and reducing lock contention. The PCP refill process is divided into four phases: recovering owned fragments, claiming whole pageblocks, grabbing sub-pageblock chunks, and traditional __rmqueue() with migratetype fallback. This approach enables efficient merging of buddy pages at the PCP level and reduces fragmentation by allowing new allocations to be packed next to existing ones in already-committed blocks.

review_comments.author: Rik Riel
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Rik Riel questioned whether having separate list heads for each migratetype in per_cpu_pages is necessary, given that the current implementation already skips over blocks of the wrong type.
review_comments.raw_body:
GIven that you just skip over blocks of the wrong migratetype,
I wonder if it makes sense to have a different list head for each
migratetype in the per_cpu_pages struct.

Not that I should be saying anything that would slow down
the merging of these patches, since making the buddy allocator
more of a slow path is pretty much a prerequisite for the 1GB
allocation stuff I'm working on :)


-- 
All Rights Reversed.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a potential concern about the size of the list, but stated that it's small in practice and didn't see any issues; they also mentioned that adding extra list heads would be feasible if needed.
review_comments.raw_body:
The list is fairly small in practice (pcp->high /
average_free_pages_per_block). I haven't run into any issues with it
so far (although like I said, my tests have been limited).

That said, I also don't think it would be an issue to do that split if
necessary. Additional list heads are 16 bytes per cpu per zone.

Let me keep an eye on this during larger scale tests.

review_comments.author: Frank Linden
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the change to rmqueue_bulk will prefer MIGRATE_MOVABLE over MIGRATE_CMA, potentially leading to an imbalance in memory allocation due to the lack of a separate PCP list for CMA pages.
sentiment_signals: lack of testing with large CMA zones
review_comments.raw_body:
Since the migrate type passed to rmqueue_bulk, where these changes
are, is the PCP migratetype, this will prefer MIGRATE_MOVABLE more
than before in the presence of MIGRATE_CMA pageblocks, right?

Currently, the CMA fallback is done when > 50% of free zone memory is
MIGRATE_CMA. For a PCP list, this isn't strictly true of course, since
grabbing a page of the PCP list doesn't do this check, and MIGRATE_CMA
doesn't have its own PCP list. But since rmqueue_bulk does do it, I'm
guessing the fallback still mostly adheres to that 50%.

With this change to rmqueue_bulk, it feels like it would prefer
MIGRATE_MOVABLE more, since that is the mt passed to it (never
MIGRATE_CMA), and the fallback is only done if the final phase is
needed.

Have you tested this with a zone that has a large amount of CMA in it
and checked the percentages?

- Frank

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: The author acknowledged issues in Phases 0 and 2 of the PCP refill process related to CMA (Contiguous Memory Allocation) handling, specifically failing to recover CMA blocks and incorrectly scanning freelists based on requested type.
review_comments.raw_body:
Good catch. Yes, I think there are problems here wrt CMA:

Phase 0 does not recover CMA blocks when movable is requested. That
looks buggy. It should restore both block types.

Phase 1 grabbing whole new blocks actually does use __rmqueue(), so it
gets the CMA fallback.

Phase 2 scans freelists based on requested type. This looks buggy as
well. It should use the logic from the to of __rmqueue() to decide
whether to grab CMA chunks instead.

Phase 3 is the regular __rmqueue() path again, which honors it.

It doesn't look hard to fix, but I'll be sure to test that.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new page allocator called pcp buddy allocator, designed to improve scalability and reduce contention in high-thread-count and large-memory systems. The current PCP (Page Cache Pool) has limitations when dealing with concurrent allocations and frees, leading to zone->lock contention. This patch addresses these issues by allowing the PCP to grab entire pageblocks, split them outside the zone->lock, and take ownership of the block. This approach reduces lock contention, increases reuse of pages, and allows for upfront merging of buddies under the local pcp->lock. However, it also introduces concerns about fragmentation, which are mitigated by remembering partial blocks on drain and queuing their pages last on the zone freelist.

review_comments.author: Zi Yan
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Zi Yan suggested an alternative approach to reduce zone->lock held time, proposing that the page-allocator control (PCP) handle free page merging before acquiring the zone->lock and returning pages to the buddy system.

Reviewer Zi Yan noted that the worst-case scenario for distributed buddy allocators is when physically contiguous free pages are allocated across all CPUs, causing all CPUs to compete for a single PCP lock, and suggested a simpler approach might work instead.

Reviewer Zi Yan suggested moving the buddy allocator out of the zone->lock and into the PCP lock, allowing one PCP to steal a whole pageblock from another if it runs out of free pages.
review_comments.raw_body:
IIUC, zone->lock held time is mostly spent on free page merging.
Have you tried to let PCP do the free page merging before holding
zone->lock and returning free pages to buddy? That is a much smaller
change than what you proposed. This method might not work if
physically contiguous free pages are allocated by separate CPUs,
so that PCP merging cannot be done. But this might be rare?

---

This is basically distributed buddy allocators, right? Instead of
relying on a single zone->lock, PCP locks are used. The worst case
it can face is that physically contiguous free pages are allocated
across all CPUs, so that all CPUs are competing a single PCP lock.
It seems that you have not hit this. So I wonder if what I proposed
above might work as a simpler approach. Let me know if I miss anything.

I wonder how this distributed buddy allocators would work if anyone
wants to allocate >pageblock free pages, like alloc_contig_range().
Multiple PCP locks need to be taken one by one. Maybe it is better
than taking and dropping zone->lock repeatedly. Have you benchmarked
alloc_contig_range(), like hugetlb allocation?

---

I wonder if we could go more radical by moving buddy allocator out of
zone->lock completely to PCP lock. If one PCP runs out of free pages,
it can steal another PCP's whole pageblock. I probably should do some
literature investigation on this. Some research must have been done
on this.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author addressed Zi Yan's concern about potential fragmentation issues due to partial blocks being remembered on drain and their pages queued last on the zone freelist, explaining that block ownership is more natural for affinity routing and increasing merge odds.

The author acknowledges that the worst-case scenario for their patch is one CPU allocating for everybody else in the system, but claims it results in mostly neutral performance over baseline, and points out that the new scheme reduces work performed under the contended lock.

Author responded to Zi Yan's concern about the PCPs being distributed, clarifying that they are still the same size and PCP pages are skipped by isolation code; no fix planned.

Author acknowledges Zi Yan's concern about stealing order and routing back frees to nominal owner, agrees that per-cpu accounting is needed, but thinks current approach of draining all CPUs' caches is inefficient.
sentiment_signals: agrees with the need for changes
review_comments.raw_body:
On my 32G system, pcp->high_min for zone Normal is 988. That's one
block and a half. The rmqueue_smallest policy means the next CPU will
prefer the remainder of that partial block. So if there is
concurrency, every other block is shared. Not exactly uncommon. The
effect lessens the larger the machine is, of course.

But let's assume it's not an issue. How do you know you can safely
merge with a buddy pfn? You need to establish that it's on that same
PCP's list. Short of *scanning* the list, it seems something like
PagePCPBuddy() and page->pcp_cpu is inevitably needed. But of course a
per-page cpu field is tough to come by.

So the block ownership is more natural, and then you might as well use
that for affinity routing to increase the odds of merges.

IOW, I'm having a hard time seeing what could be taken away and still
have it work.

---

The worst case is one CPU allocating for everybody else in the system,
so that all freers route to that PCP.

I've played with microbenchmarks to provoke this, but it looks mostly
neutral over baseline, at least at the scale of this machine.

In this scenario, baseline will have the affinity mismatch problem:
the allocating CPU routinely hits zone->lock to refill, and the
freeing CPUs routinely hit zone->lock to drain and merge.

In the new scheme, they would hit the pcp->lock instead of the
zone->lock. So not necessarily an improvement in lock breaking. BUT
because freers refill the allocator's cache, merging is deferred;
that's a net reduction of work performed under the contended lock.

---

I didn't change that aspect.

The PCPs are still the same size, and PCP pages are still skipped by
the isolation code.

IOW it's not a purely distributed buddy allocator. It's still just a
per-cpu cache of limited size. The only thing I'm doing is provide a
mechanism for splitting and pre-merging at the cache level, and
setting up affinity/routing rules to increase the chances of
success. But the impact on alloc_contig should be the same.

---

This is an interesting idea. Make the zone buddy a pure block economy
and remove all buddy code from it. Slowpath allocs and frees would
always be in whole blocks.

You'd have to come up with a natural stealing order. If one CPU needs
something it doesn't have, which CPUs, and which order, do you look at
for stealing.

I think you'd still have to route back frees to the nominal owner of
the block, or stealing could scatter pages all over the place and we'd
never be able to merge them back up.

I think you'd also need to pull accounting (NR_FREE_PAGES) to the
per-cpu level, and inform compaction/isolation to deal with these
pages, since the majority default is now distributed.

But the scenario where one CPU needs what another one has is an
interesting one. I didn't invent anything new for this for now, but
rather rely on how we have been handling this through the zone
freelists. But I do think it's a little silly: right now, if a CPU
needs something another CPU might have, we ask EVERY CPU in the system
to drain their cache into the shared pool - simultaneously - running
the full buddy merge algorithm on everything that comes in. The CPU
grabs a small handful of these pages, most likely having to split
again. All other CPUs are now cache cold on the next request.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: The author acknowledged issues with CMA (Contiguous Memory Allocation) handling in the patch and agreed to fix them, stating it doesn't look hard to do so.
review_comments.raw_body:
Good catch. Yes, I think there are problems here wrt CMA:

Phase 0 does not recover CMA blocks when movable is requested. That
looks buggy. It should restore both block types.

Phase 1 grabbing whole new blocks actually does use __rmqueue(), so it
gets the CMA fallback.

Phase 2 scans freelists based on requested type. This looks buggy as

review_comments.author: Zi Yan
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Zi Yan noted that pages that can be merged are not typically freed by the same CPU, which was an assumption made in the patch, and requested clarification on this point.

Reviewer Zi Yan suggested that the zone buddy could keep track of PCP free lists for stealing, but this idea was presented as naive and not further developed.

Reviewer Zi Yan suggested implementing a mechanism for merging free pages across all Protection Classes (PCPs) to maximize page coalescing.

Reviewer suggested a different approach to distributed buddy allocation, where CPUs can request other CPUs to drain minimal free pages, but noted that they don't have a good idea on how to implement this yet.
sentiment_signals: acknowledged current approach as a good first step
review_comments.raw_body:
You are right. I was assuming that pages that can be merged are freed
via the same CPU. That rarely happens.

---

One naive idea is to make zone buddy keep track of PCP free lists
for stealing.

---

Basically, we want to keep free pages to be merged as much as possible.
Something like free page compaction across all PCPs.

---

Yes, a better way might be that when a CPU wants something, it should be
able to ask the other CPUs to drain the minimal amount of free pages.
But I do not have a good idea on how to do that yet.

It sounds to me that your current approach is a good first step towards
distributed buddy allocator. I will check the code and think about it
more and ask questions later.

Thank you for the explanation.

Best Regards,
Yan, Zi

The PCP is a per-cpu cache of limited size that stores pages to reduce contention on the zone lock. This approach aims to improve performance by reducing lock contention and deferring merging, but it does not change the underlying block economy or buddy code. The impact on alloc_contig should remain the same.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the vmpressure() function to skip socket pressure assertion when reclaim is triggered by high-order allocations on a fragmented system. The patch introduces an order-aware parameter that allows vmpressure() to differentiate between costly and non-costly orders, asserting socket pressure only for orders above PAGE_ALLOC_COSTLY_ORDER. This change aims to prevent unnecessary throttling of TCP throughput due to poor reclaim efficiency at high orders, while maintaining the existing behavior for memcg reclaim and low-priority reclaim scenarios.

review_comments.author: Barry Song
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Barry Song noted that while costly order allocations should be treated as costly for reclamation, nominally non-costly orders (e.g., order-3) can also become costly due to burst network traffic, and suggested revisiting the discussion soon.
review_comments.raw_body:
This patch looks sensible to me

Reviewed-by: Barry Song <baohua@kernel.org>

This is a one-sided costly order and should be treated as costly for
reclamation. On the other hand, nominally non-costly orders (e.g.,
order-3) can also become costly. I previously raised this issue here:

https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20251013101636.69220-1-21cnbao@gmail.com/

Burst network traffic can make phones heat up.

More recently, with help from Wang Lian and Kunwu Chan (Cc'ed),
we developed a simple model that reliably reproduces this
behavior, where we observe significant CPU utilization by
kswapd due to 3-order burst allocation from the network.

I may revisit this discussion soon.

Best Regards
Barry

review_comments.summary: Expressed enthusiasm for the contribution and suggested including a clear description of userspace benefits in the changelog.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, 6 Apr 2026 12:07:57 -0700 "JP Kobryn (Meta)" <jp.kobryn@linux.dev> wrote:

> Let me know if this data helps, and if you'd like this added to the
> changelog.
> 
> On one affected host with impacted net throughput, the memory state at
> the time showed ~15GB available, zero cgroup pressure, and the following
> buddyinfo state:
> 
> Order FreePages
> 0:    133,970
> 1:    29,230
> 2:    17,351
> 3:    18,984
> 7+:   0
> 
> Using bpf, it was found that 94% of vmpressure calls on this host were
> from order-7 kswapd reclaim.
> 
> TCP minimum recv window is rcv_ssthresh:19712.
> 
> Before patch:
> 723 out of 3,843 (19%) TCP connections stuck at minimum recv window
> 
> After live-patching and ~30min elapsed:
> 0 out of 3,470 TCP connections stuck at minimum recv window

Well I'm impressed ;)

Yes please, it's a useful thing to include.  When people look at a
contribution, question #1 is "why should I spend time on this".  A
clear (and tasty) description of userspace benefit is the ideal way of
answering that.
